verdicts.title: array_map(intval) flagged as RCE
verdicts.rationale: Source is `array_map(function($item){return intval($item);}, $attributes['selectedFolder'])`. intval() is a strong cast — output is always a PHP integer, never user-controllable string. No shell execution in the chain. The analyzer incorrectly classified `array_map` as an RCE sink. Not a vulnerability.

verdicts.title: SQL injection in gallery block render.php WHERE clause
verdicts.rationale: File: blocks/filebird-gallery/build/render.php line 12-14. The folder IDs are first passed through `array_map(intval, ...)` so every value is a PHP integer before being imploded into the query. Integers in SQL context are safe without prepare(). Additionally this code runs inside a Gutenberg block render callback — the block attributes come from the post content which only admins/editors can save. Not exploitable.

verdicts.title: HTTP_REFERER parsed via parse_str
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Classes/Tree.php line 94-98. parse_str() on a user-controlled HTTP_REFERER is generally concerning (variable injection), but the context is an admin-only page (auth_required=true per the taint analysis) and the resulting variables are used for informational breadcrumb/tab-switching purposes. The scope of $referer_params is local and not used in SQL or HTML output without further processing. Medium-risk at best; requires authenticated admin session.

verdicts.title: XSS via $_GET['fbv'] in select dropdown
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Classes/Core.php line 163/177. The source is `(int) sanitize_text_field($_GET['fbv'])` — the (int) cast makes the value a PHP integer regardless of input. It is then output inside `selected(intval($folder->id), $fbv, false)` which compares two integers and echoes 'selected' or nothing. The `%1$d` format specifier in printf also enforces integer output. Not exploitable.

verdicts.title: RCE via post_type in PostType addon
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Addons/PostType/Controllers/Main.php line 17-48. The value is `sanitize_text_field($request->get_param('post_type'))`. The chain ends at `array_map(fn($term) => MainModel::convertFormat($term, $colors), $top_level_terms)` — this is data transformation using PHP array_map, not a shell command. No shell execution in path. FALSE POSITIVE.

verdicts.title: RCE via get_option fbv_enabled_posttype
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Admin/Settings.php line 75-79. Source is `get_option('fbv_enabled_posttype')`, sink is `array_filter(..., fn($pt) => $pt === 'post' || $pt === 'page')`. The filter allowlist only accepts 'post' or 'page'. No shell execution. FALSE POSITIVE.

verdicts.title: RCE via $_POST['post_types'] in Settings
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Admin/Settings.php line 169-170. Source is `Helpers::sanitize_array($_POST['post_types'])`, sink is `array_filter(..., fn($pt) => $pt === 'post' || $pt === 'page')`. Allowlist whitelisting, no shell execution. Already authenticated + nonce verified (auth_required=true, nonce_verified=true). FALSE POSITIVE.

verdicts.title: RCE via usort in SyncController
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Controller/SyncController.php line 104. `usort($folders, fn($a,$b) => intval($a['ord']) > intval($b['ord']) ? 1 : -1)`. This is standard PHP sorting with intval casts. No shell execution. FALSE POSITIVE.

verdicts.title: RCE via userId in SyncController
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Controller/SyncController.php line 138. `intval($request->get_param('userId'))` piped into `array_filter`. No shell execution. Data filtered only. auth_required=true. FALSE POSITIVE.

verdicts.title: XSS via folder name in API create response
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Controller/Api.php line 105-109. `sanitize_text_field($request->get_param('name'))` flows into `FolderModel::newUniqueFolder($name, $parent_id)` and the response is `wp_send_json_success(array('id' => $folder['id']))`. Only the folder ID (integer) is echoed in the JSON response, not the name. JSON encoding in wp_send_json_success also escapes all HTML entities. NOT exploitable for XSS.

verdicts.title: SQLi via drag_node name flow to wpdb->update
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Controller/FolderController.php. The initial $drag_node is fetched with $wpdb->prepare() (line 136). The name then flows to FolderModel::findUniqueFolderName() then to $wpdb->update() with format specifier '%s'. $wpdb->update() uses internal escaping for the data array (equivalent to prepare). Auth required, not a real injection path.

verdicts.title: SQLi via get_term_meta to wpdb->insert
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Controller/Convert.php line 30,35. `$parent = get_term_meta($parent, 'new_fbv_id', true)`. The taint analysis marks get_term_meta as a source but this is data from WordPress's own term meta table (trusted). The sink is `$wpdb->insert()` which uses internal parameter binding. Not exploitable as SQL injection. Converter only runs during admin migration.

verdicts.title: RCE via usort with dynamic method name
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Model/Folder.php line 46-49. `usort($folders, array(__CLASS__, "sort_natural_$order"))` — the $order variable is set to either 'asc' or 'desc' earlier in the function using a strict comparison/whitelist. The method names `sort_natural_asc` and `sort_natural_desc` are both defined in the class. Not user-influenced. FALSE POSITIVE.

verdicts.title: SQLi via updateFolderName sanitized flow to wpdb->update
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Model/Folder.php line 357-369. `sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash(wp_kses_post($new_name)))` flows to `$wpdb->update(..., array('name' => $new_name), ..., array('%s'), array('%d'))`. $wpdb->update() uses internal escaping analogous to prepare(). Not SQLi.

verdicts.title: SQLi via findUniqueFolderName to wpdb->update
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Model/Folder.php line 357-383. Same pattern as sqli-004. All string values pass through $wpdb->update() with '%s' format. Not exploitable.

verdicts.title: SQLi via get_var prepare chain to wpdb->insert
verdicts.rationale: File: includes/Model/Folder.php line 487-499. Source is a safely prepared SELECT MAX(ord) query result (an integer). This integer is then used in $wpdb->insert() with '%d' format. Not SQLi.

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated folder creation and attachment reassignment via Public REST API with API key bearer token
verdicts.description: FileBird registers public REST routes at /wp-json/filebird/public/v1/ including POST /folders (creates a new folder) and POST /folder/set-attachment (reassigns media attachments to folders). These routes use a custom bearer-token auth scheme (permission_callback checks a 40-char API key stored in option fbv_rest_api_key). If the API key is generated and publicly known, any unauthenticated user can create folders and reassign media library attachments. The key is generated client-side via POST /wp-json/filebird/v1/fbv-api?act=generate-key (admin only) but if leaked, allows full media library folder manipulation without a WordPress session.
verdicts.notes: Requires knowledge of the API key. The key mechanism is intentional (documented public API) but the create-folder and set-attachment endpoints grant write access which should require at minimum upload_files capability. The endpoints at /wp-json/filebird/public/v1/folders (POST), /folder/set-attachment (POST) accept requests with only a bearer token header, no WordPress authentication.

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated read access to media library folder structure via Public REST API
verdicts.description: GET /wp-json/filebird/public/v1/folders, /wp-json/filebird/public/v1/folder?folder_id=X, and /wp-json/filebird/public/v1/attachment-id?folder_id=X all require only a bearer token API key, not a WordPress user session. These endpoints expose the complete folder tree, folder names, and all attachment IDs organized by folder — which may contain private media. The permission_callback returns false if no 40-char key exists, but if a key has been generated, the response is fully open. Since the key is stored in wp_options (fbv_rest_api_key) and is sometimes embedded in page source for JS integrations, it may be accessible to unauthenticated users.
verdicts.notes: This is by design (it's a public API), but the information disclosure of attachment IDs for a private media library is a security concern. The key must be obtained somehow (e.g., found in JS enqueued on frontend, etc.).

verdicts.title: Semgrep: REST routes without permission_callback in Routes.php, Convert.php, ImportController.php
verdicts.rationale: Semgrep flagged these due to a pattern mismatch. All routes in Addons/PostType/Routes.php have 'permission_callback' => array($this, 'resPermissionsCheck') checking manage_options||edit_posts. All routes in Classes/Convert.php have 'permission_callback' => array($this, 'resPermissionsCheck') checking upload_files. ImportController.php permission_callback checks upload_files. These are all authenticated-only routes. The semgrep rule appears to have been triggered by the pattern not including the expected string literal. No auth bypass present.

verdicts.title: Direct $wpdb->get_results call with variable in PostType/Controllers/Main.php
verdicts.description: Lines 412 and 506 in includes/Addons/PostType/Controllers/Main.php pass variables directly to $wpdb query methods. Requires reviewing the specific queries to determine if user input reaches them. Routes are auth-protected (manage_options||edit_posts). Even if injectable, exploitation requires editor-level auth.
verdicts.verdict_note: Lower priority — authenticated route, likely FP. Not promoted to CONFIRMED without reading the specific code.

summary.key_finding: All automated taint-flow RCE/SQLi findings are false positives (intval casts, $wpdb->update with format specifiers, internal data sources). Two legitimate issues found: the intentional public REST API can be abused if the API key is known to create folders and reassign attachments (medium) and disclose folder/attachment metadata (low-medium). No unauthenticated SQL injection or RCE found.
